{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48670","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48670/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48670/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48670/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48670","id":1584218583,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5ebUHX","number":48670,"title":"Stackframe missing when using keyword arguments ","user":{"login":"kdheepak","id":1813121,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4MTMxMjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1813121?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak","html_url":"https://github.com/kdheepak","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-02-14T14:00:46Z","updated_at":"2023-02-14T14:00:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I've able to reproduce a case consistently where a stackframe is missing when the error is reported. I've uploaded a minimal working example (MWE) here: https://github.com/kdheepak/TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE/tree/0d5b28acdd8836078c17d42a4668da7588f9cfc7

The MWE has two functions, one where the issue occurs `main1()` and another where the issue does not occur `main2()`. 

The only difference between `main1()` and `main2()` is the following:

```
main1() = (; data1=Data(; year=1))
main2() = (; data1=Data())
```

I've also defined a function that throws an error:

```
default_value(name::String) = throw("$name not found")
```

which is called in the keyword argument constructor for the `Data` struct, causing it to error.

The `Data` struct is defined like so in the `src/data.jl` file.

```
struct Data
  x1::Vector{String}
end

Data(; x1=default_value("x1")) = Data(x1)
```

Here's the output of running `main1()`:

```julia
$ julia --project -e 'using TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE; TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.main1()'
ERROR: "x1 not found"
Stacktrace:
 [1] default_value(name::String)
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:2
 [2] main1()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:4
 [3] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

Here's the output of running `main2()`:

```julia
$ julia --project -e 'using TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE; TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.main2()'
ERROR: "x1 not found"
Stacktrace:
 [1] default_value(name::String)
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:2
 [2] TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.Data()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\data.jl:5
 [3] main2()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:6
 [4] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

When running `main1()` the stacktrace omits the line contains the call into the `Data` struct constructor, that is to say the `struct Data ... end` definition and constructor is in `src/data.jl` but there's no line in the stacktrace to `src/data.jl`.

This missing stackframe is present in `main2()`, which shows `TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.Data()` being called in `src/data.jl`.

---

I installed Julia using the official installer online. I'm using Julia 1.8.5 on Windows:

```
julia> versioninfo()
```

The `Data` struct is defined like so in the `src/data.jl` file.

```julia
struct Data
  x1::Vector{String}
end

Data(; x1=default_value("x1")) = Data(x1)
```

Note here that the `year` keyword is not being used. But this issue also occurs if the struct definition is this instead:

```julia
struct Data
  year::Int
  x1::Vector{String}
end

Data(; year=1, x1=default_value("x1")) = Data(year, x1)

# OR

Base.@kwdef struct Data
  year::Int = 1
  x1::Vector{String} = default_value("x1")
end
```

Here's the output of running `main1()`:

```julia
$ julia --project -e 'using TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE; TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.main1()'
ERROR: "x1 not found"
Stacktrace:
 [1] default_value(name::String)
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:2
 [2] main1()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:4
 [3] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

Here's the output of running `main2()`:

```julia
$ julia --project -e 'using TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE; TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.main2()'
ERROR: "x1 not found"
Stacktrace:
 [1] default_value(name::String)
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:2
 [2] TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.Data()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\data.jl:5
 [3] main2()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:6
 [4] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

When running `main1()` the stacktrace omits the line contains the call into the `Data` struct constructor, that is to say the `struct Data ... end` definition and constructor is in `src/data.jl` but there's no line in the stacktrace that points to a call site in `src/data.jl`.

This missing stackframe is present in `main2()`, which shows `TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.Data()` being called with the call site in `src/data.jl`.

---

I installed Julia using the official installer online. I'm using Julia 1.8.5 on Windows:

```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.8.5
Commit 17cfb8e65e (2023-01-08 06:45 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Windows (x86_64-w64-mingw32)
  CPU: 8 × 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-1145G7 @ 2.60GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-13.0.1 (ORCJIT, tigerlake)
  Threads: 1 on 8 virtual cores
```

Here's a even more simpler example. 

Create two files: `src/main.jl` and `src/foo.jl`:

```julia
# src/main.jl
default_value() = throw("oh no")

main1() = foo(; kwarg_not_used=1)
main2() = foo()
```

```julia
# src/foo.jl
foo(; x1=default_value()) = nothing
```

These are the stacktraces for `main1()` and `main2()`:

```julia
$ julia --project -e 'using TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE; TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.main1()'
ERROR: "oh no"
Stacktrace:
 [1] default_value()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:2
 [2] main1()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:4
 [3] top-level scope
   @ none:1

$ julia --project -e 'using TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE; TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE.main2()'
ERROR: "oh no"
Stacktrace:
 [1] default_value()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:2
 [2] foo()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\foo.jl:1
 [3] main2()
   @ TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE C:\Users\USERNAME\gitrepos\TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE\src\main.jl:6
 [4] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

It appears that just passing in a `kwarg_not_used` causes the call order of functions to change. 

My best guess for what is happening here is that in `main2()` the `foo()` function is called and because of calling the `foo` function it calls `default_value()` which errors, but in `main1()` the `default_value()` function is called first, and the `foo()` function never appears in the stacktrace.

I've shared the MWE for this here: https://github.com/kdheepak/TestInlineErrorOmissionMWE/tree/80c1d80d5c475299ea7ecf2374c0f5c23bb0ca85

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48740","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48740/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48740/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48740/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48740","id":1592681999,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5e7mYP","number":48740,"title":"Improve error message when in incorrect environment and loading package","user":{"login":"kdheepak","id":1813121,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4MTMxMjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1813121?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak","html_url":"https://github.com/kdheepak","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kdheepak/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-02-21T02:28:51Z","updated_at":"2023-02-21T02:28:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello! I have a suggestion to improve the error message that appears when a user tries to load a package in the wrong environment, with a focus on making it more beginner-friendly.

When working with beginners, I usually write documentation in the form of a tutorial and often provide a set of steps for them to follow:

```
1) git clone https://github.com/username/tutorial
2) cd tutorial
3) julia --project 
4) julia> using Pkg; Pkg.instantiate()
5) julia> import Foo
```

If someone forgets to `cd tutorial` or accidentally runs the `julia` command without the `--project` flag, or even opens the Julia app and `cd`s to the right folder, the default environment is loaded up. And when they run `import Foo` this is the error message provided:

```julia
julia> import Foo
ERROR: ArgumentError: Package Foo not found in current path.
- Run `import Pkg; Pkg.add("Foo")` to install the Foo package.
```

Usually at this point, people share the error message back to me and I have no idea what went wrong. I've even seen a couple of people try to run `import Pkg; Pkg.add("Foo")` at this point because of the hint suggestion.

To make it easier for beginners to understand what is happening, it would be great if the error message included information about the current environment. For example, if the error message was the following:

```
julia> import Foo
ERROR: ArgumentError: Package Foo not found in current path (C:\path\to\projects) or current environment (@v1.8).
- Tip: You can `import Pkg; Pkg.add("Foo")` to install the Foo package into the current environment.
```

it might be immediately obvious even to user as to what is going on, and they wouldn't have to reach out to me to begin with. If they did reach out to me, it would be more clear to me what was happening.

I also think it would be helpful to display the current environment in the REPL prompt by default. This error message would still be an improvement over the status quo:

```julia
(@v1.8) julia> import Foo
ERROR: ArgumentError: Package Foo not found in current path.
- Run `import Pkg; Pkg.add("Foo")` to install the Foo package.
```

Even a single character to show a environment is active (e.g. `julia*>`) would be instructive. This would be particularly useful for beginners, who I would like to not burden with the task of customizing the prompt in the `startup.jl` file right off the bat.

What do people think about this suggestion?

This is not really an issue, but a couple of projects I found could be helpful and inspire features. For example, I used `using Base64` when prototyping in a REPL and then copied the code over to my package. I got the following error message:

```julia
julia> using Package
[ Info: Precompiling Package [c708b252-dc17-4f71-991d-7f58ea29e49c]
ERROR: LoadError: ArgumentError: Package Package does not have Base64 in its dependencies:
- You may have a partially installed environment. Try `Pkg.instantiate()`
  to ensure all packages in the environment are installed.
- Or, if you have Package checked out for development and have
  added Base64 as a dependency but haven't updated your primary
  environment's manifest file, try `Pkg.resolve()`.
- Otherwise you may need to report an issue with Package
Stacktrace:
 [1] macro expansion
   @ ./loading.jl:1051 [inlined]
```
